nodes.label: Agentic Time Series Forecasting for Supervillains: Proposed Outline
nodes.norm_label: agentic time series forecasting for supervillains: proposed outline

nodes.label: Chapter 1: Introducing Omen and Agentic AI
nodes.norm_label: chapter 1: introducing omen and agentic ai

nodes.label: Chapter 2: Installing Omen (OpenClaw, Claude Code, Hermes, and Friends)
nodes.norm_label: chapter 2: installing omen (openclaw, claude code, hermes, and friends)

nodes.label: Chapter 3: First Contact — Loading Data and Basic Statistics
nodes.norm_label: chapter 3: first contact — loading data and basic statistics

nodes.label: Chapter 4: Is This Thing Stable? — Stationarity, ADF & KPSS
nodes.norm_label: chapter 4: is this thing stable? — stationarity, adf & kpss

nodes.label: Chapter 5: The Rhythm of Evil — Seasonality Detection & Decomposition
nodes.norm_label: chapter 5: the rhythm of evil — seasonality detection & decomposition

nodes.label: Chapter 6: Echoes — Autocorrelation and the ACF/PACF
nodes.norm_label: chapter 6: echoes — autocorrelation and the acf/pacf

nodes.label: Chapter 7: Spikes and Regime Changes — Anomaly and Changepoint Detection
nodes.norm_label: chapter 7: spikes and regime changes — anomaly and changepoint detection

nodes.label: Chapter 8: The Floor Is Naive — Baselines You Must Beat
nodes.norm_label: chapter 8: the floor is naive — baselines you must beat

nodes.label: Agentic AI (typed-tool-calling loop)
nodes.rationale: Defined narrowly as an AI reasoning about which pre-built, typed tool to call next and reading structured results back, in contrast to an LLM freehand-writing analysis code from memory — the latter never checks stationarity, never reports a confidence interval, and never flags that it should be doubted.

nodes.label: Deterministic gates around agentic judgment
nodes.rationale: Central design tension of the book: agent judgment is valuable for open-ended reasoning (which model family fits this series?) and dangerous for consequential yes/no actions (should this go to production now?). Omen's layer boundaries are drawn deliberately along that fault line, e.g. compare_candidate_to_deployed and execute_redeploy's confirmed=True requirement.

nodes.label: Five-layer pipeline (explore, backtest, deploy, monitor, retrain)
nodes.rationale: Chapters escalate in the same order the five Omen layers were designed to be used, because you can't honestly evaluate a model until you've looked at the data, and can't honestly monitor a model until you've deployed one.
nodes.norm_label: five-layer pipeline (explore, backtest, deploy, monitor, retrain)

nodes.label: Subprocess PATH / venv installation gotcha
nodes.rationale: A subprocess launched by a bare command name (e.g. ts-analyst-server) can fail with FileNotFoundError because it doesn't inherit the parent process's activated-venv PATH, even though the same command works in an interactive terminal. Fix: use the absolute path to the installed console script.

nodes.label: Never report a number without reporting how sure you are
nodes.rationale: A rule that holds everywhere in Omen without exception, first shown via basic_stats returning mean_ci_lower/mean_ci_upper alongside mean; later demonstrated by null confidence intervals on constant series rather than a fabricated zero-width interval.
nodes.norm_label: never report a number without reporting how sure you are

nodes.label: Student's t confidence interval
nodes.rationale: mean ± t(0.975, df=n-1) x (std/sqrt(n)); standard error must use the count of non-missing values, not the raw row count, or the reported interval is silently narrower than the data supports.

nodes.label: ADF test (Augmented Dickey-Fuller)
nodes.rationale: Null hypothesis: the series has a unit root (non-stationary); a low p-value is evidence for stationarity. Deliberately paired with KPSS's opposite null so agreement/disagreement itself is informative.

nodes.label: KPSS test (Kwiatkowski-Phillips-Schmidt-Shin)
nodes.rationale: Null hypothesis: the series is stationary (opposite of ADF). Its p-value is interpolated from four lookup-table points and clipped at the edges, so kpss_effect_size (statistic as a multiple of its 5% critical value) is needed to distinguish a barely-crossed result from an unambiguous one.

nodes.label: Mean-reversion half-life / lambda
nodes.rationale: A series can clear p<0.05 on ADF while reverting so slowly that stationarity is technically true but practically useless for a 30-day forecast. Under a genuine unit root, the OLS lambda estimate skews slightly negative in finite samples even with zero true reversion, so a small negative lambda alone is not proof of real mean-reversion.

nodes.label: Fisher's g-test
nodes.rationale: Tests whether the periodogram's single strongest candidate frequency is stronger than pure noise would produce, rather than just being the biggest number in the list. Omen uses a standard conservative approximation, not the full exact formula.

nodes.label: Trend overwhelming seasonality in a periodogram
nodes.rationale: A strong, sustained trend behaves spectrally like an extremely long, slow cycle (period near the full series length), so the globally strongest periodogram frequency can be the trend rather than real seasonality. A sufficiently dominant trend can distort the whole periodogram badly enough to bury the real signal even among in-range candidates, which is why stationarity checks (Ch.4) belong before seasonality search.
nodes.norm_label: trend overwhelming seasonality in a periodogram

nodes.label: PACF (partial autocorrelation function)
nodes.rationale: On a strongly persistent series, PACF estimation can become numerically unstable beyond lag 1, returning values mathematically outside the valid [-1,1] range — not a bug, but a sign the series needs differencing first.

nodes.label: Bartlett's formula (lag-dependent ACF significance threshold)
nodes.rationale: The correct significance threshold for a lag grows with earlier lags rather than staying flat at 1.96/sqrt(n); a flat threshold matches the correct one only at lag 1 and otherwise both misses real structure and creates false positives further out. Confidence intervals are centered on the estimated ACF value itself, not on zero.

nodes.label: Self-dilution in rolling z-score anomaly detection
nodes.rationale: A rolling mean/std window that includes the anomalous point itself has its own standard deviation inflated by that same point, so the reported z-score comes out smaller than the anomaly deserves — a structural weakness of mean/std-based detection generically, fixed by a robust median/MAD-based alternative.
nodes.norm_label: self-dilution in rolling z-score anomaly detection

nodes.label: Modified z-score / MAD (median absolute deviation)
nodes.rationale: Robust alternative to mean/std z-scores, built from rolling median and MAD; resistant to self-dilution but can flag ordinary noise as unusual during a quiet stretch with a tight local baseline.

nodes.label: Permutation test (for changepoint p-values)
nodes.rationale: Assigns a p-value by repeatedly shuffling the series and recomputing the CUSUM statistic, avoiding a closed-form distributional assumption; each changepoint's p-value is a local test, not a global significance guarantee across all detected breaks.

nodes.label: Seasonal-naive baseline forecast
nodes.rationale: Repeats the value from one full seasonal cycle back; on a trending series with no real seasonality, this can be worse than flat naive because it anchors to an older, systematically lower point.

nodes.label: MAPE (mean absolute percentage error)
nodes.rationale: Undefined at an actual value of exactly zero; excluded points are reported explicitly (mape_points_excluded_near_zero) rather than silently dropped.

nodes.label: Bootstrap confidence interval
nodes.rationale: Built by resampling with replacement rather than a closed-form formula; two models' point-estimate MAPEs can look decisive while their bootstrap CIs overlap substantially, which is not yet proof of a reliable difference (Diebold-Mariano in Ch.12 answers that properly).

nodes.label: Cohen's d (standardized effect size for changepoints/drift)
nodes.rationale: The glossary explicitly notes this is a different, unrelated concept from Ch.4's own 'effect size' entries (mean-reversion half-life, KPSS ratio), sharing only the name.
nodes.norm_label: cohen's d (standardized effect size for changepoints/drift)

nodes.label: Dickey & Fuller (1979) — Distribution of the Estimators for Autoregressive Time Series with a Unit Root
nodes.norm_label: dickey & fuller (1979) — distribution of the estimators for autoregressive time series with a unit root

nodes.label: Kwiatkowski, Phillips, Schmidt & Shin (1992) — Testing the Null Hypothesis of Stationarity
nodes.norm_label: kwiatkowski, phillips, schmidt & shin (1992) — testing the null hypothesis of stationarity

nodes.label: Fisher (1929) — Tests of Significance in Harmonic Analysis
nodes.norm_label: fisher (1929) — tests of significance in harmonic analysis

nodes.label: Bartlett (1946) — On the Theoretical Specification and Sampling Properties of Autocorrelated Time-Series
nodes.norm_label: bartlett (1946) — on the theoretical specification and sampling properties of autocorrelated time-series

nodes.label: Iglewicz & Hoya (1993) — How to Detect and Handle Outliers
nodes.norm_label: iglewicz & hoya (1993) — how to detect and handle outliers

nodes.label: Hurvich & Tsai (1989) — Regression and Time Series Model Selection in Small Samples
nodes.norm_label: hurvich & tsai (1989) — regression and time series model selection in small samples

nodes.label: Hyndman & Athanasopoulos — Forecasting: Principles and Practice
nodes.norm_label: hyndman & athanasopoulos — forecasting: principles and practice

nodes.label: Newey & West (1994) — Automatic Lag Selection in Covariance Matrix Estimation
nodes.norm_label: newey & west (1994) — automatic lag selection in covariance matrix estimation

nodes.label: Wilson (1927) — Probable Inference, the Law of Succession, and Statistical Inference
nodes.norm_label: wilson (1927) — probable inference, the law of succession, and statistical inference

nodes.label: Cohen (1988) — Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences
nodes.norm_label: cohen (1988) — statistical power analysis for the behavioral sciences

nodes.label: Exponential Smoothing (ETS / Holt-Winters)
nodes.rationale: Builds a forecast from level, optional trend, optional seasonal components, each additive or multiplicative, trend optionally damped. Chapter 9's real fits show AIC/AICc do not reliably pick the best-forecasting configuration.

nodes.label: AIC (Akaike Information Criterion)
nodes.rationale: Balances fit against complexity; lower is better, but does not measure interval calibration or out-of-sample accuracy directly.

nodes.label: AICc (small-sample corrected AIC)
nodes.rationale: Small-sample correction to AIC (Hurvich & Tsai, 1989); AIC's bias grows as parameter count approaches sample size. In Chapter 9's real fits, the lowest-AICc model (damped trend) forecast worse and had a badly miscalibrated interval.

nodes.label: Ljung-Box Test (residual white noise check)
nodes.rationale: Fixed statistical test with a fixed critical value, not a subjective read of a residual plot; used to certify residuals_look_like_white_noise.

nodes.label: backtest_interval_coverage diagnostic
nodes.rationale: Real result: additive-seasonal ETS models showed only 36.67% empirical coverage against a 95% nominal target -- badly miscalibrated despite excellent MAPE and clean residuals, proving point accuracy and interval trustworthiness are separate questions.

nodes.label: Damped Trend (ETS parameter)
nodes.rationale: Lowest AICc (487.11 vs 494.35) of the models tried, but MAPE more than doubled to 9.50% and interval coverage collapsed to 6.67% -- a real case of AICc selecting the worse forecasting model.

nodes.label: Multiplicative Seasonal Component
nodes.rationale: Switching the seasonal component from additive to multiplicative (matching Death-Ray Revenue's percentage-scale growth) raised MAPE slightly (4.62% to 5.55%) but fixed interval calibration (36.67% to 93.33% coverage), with AIC/AICc barely moving.

nodes.label: ETS's Simulated Prediction Interval Mechanism
nodes.rationale: ETS's interval is built by simulating hundreds of future paths and taking percentiles, not a closed-form formula -- explains why an additive-noise simulation assumption produced a badly calibrated interval on a series whose variance scales with its level. Falls back to point-forecast-only with an interval_note if simulation fails.

nodes.label: SARIMA (p,d,q)(P,D,Q,s)
nodes.rationale: d grounded in Chapter 4's ADF/KPSS finding (d=1); guessed p=1,q=1 barely beat naive (15.08% MAPE); a searched (1,1,2) cut MAPE to 7.23% but still had a badly miscalibrated analytic interval (30% coverage vs 95% nominal).

nodes.label: Differencing Orders d, D
nodes.rationale: How many times the series/seasonal cycle must be differenced to be stationary; deliberately never searched by search_sarima_orders because it is a real, tested, layer-1 question (Chapter 4), not a numerically-optimized one.

nodes.label: AR/MA Orders p, q, P, Q
nodes.rationale: Autoregressive and moving-average orders and their seasonal counterparts; a reasonable, bounded search space for an automated grid search, unlike d/D.
nodes.norm_label: ar/ma orders p, q, p, q

nodes.label: search_sarima_orders tool
nodes.rationale: Advisory order search over p,q with d fixed; found (1,1,2) and (2,1,2) separated by under one AICc point (546.52 vs 547.12) and its own interpretation explicitly recommended a Diebold-Mariano test rather than trusting the ranking blind.

nodes.label: SARIMA's Analytic Prediction Interval
nodes.rationale: Derived directly from the fitted state-space model's math, no simulation -- yet still badly miscalibrated (30% coverage) on Death-Ray Revenue, proving neither model family nor interval-construction method guarantees calibration.

nodes.label: Gradient Boosted Trees (GBT) Forecaster
nodes.rationale: An ordinary tabular GBT regressor reframed for forecasting via lag/calendar features; genuinely competitive (13.00% MAPE on minion overtime) but evaluated under an easier one-step-ahead backtest regime.

nodes.label: fit_gradient_boosted_trees tool
nodes.rationale: Fixed feature set: lag_1, lag_7, lag_14, day_of_week, month, time_index -- no cyclical encoding built in.

nodes.label: Feature Importance Result
nodes.rationale: lag_7 dominated at 0.676 importance vs lag_1's 0.038, revealing the day-of-week pattern; month's importance was a nearly negligible 0.0074 despite a real year-end effect baked into the data. Feature importance measures predictive usefulness within the model, not causation.

nodes.label: Cyclical Encoding (sin/cos day-of-year)
nodes.rationale: The standard fix for handing a smooth annual cycle to a tree model: sin(2*pi*day_of_year/365.25) and cos(...) instead of a 12-bucket month integer, avoiding the artificial December-January jump. Not built into fit_gradient_boosted_trees; would need to be applied upstream.

nodes.label: One-Step-Ahead Backtest Caveat
nodes.rationale: GBT's backtest uses true lagged values at each holdout point (one-step-ahead), an easier evaluation setting than ETS/SARIMA's genuine multi-step backtests -- their MAPE numbers are not directly comparable even in the same units.

nodes.label: Diebold-Mariano Test
nodes.rationale: Compares a paired loss differential at every holdout point rather than two error numbers in isolation; resolved Chapter 8's naive-vs-seasonal-naive overlap (p=0.0002, favors naive) and Chapter 10's near-tied SARIMA orders (p=0.0083, favors (1,1,2)). Also found SARIMA decisively beats both baselines, reversing the book's originally planned upset narrative.

nodes.label: Newey-West HAC-robust variance estimate
nodes.rationale: Used because multi-step backtest errors are typically autocorrelated (an error at step 12 isn't independent of step 11); a plain t-test would understate uncertainty. Lag count chosen automatically by the Newey & West (1994) rule unless overridden.

nodes.label: n_lags Parameter Choice
nodes.rationale: Should match what the backtest actually is: default HAC lag rule for genuinely multi-step comparisons, n_lags=0 for one-step-ahead GBT comparisons -- decided by the backtest's design, not by which answer either setting gives.

nodes.label: Honest Reversal of the Planned SARIMA-Loses Narrative
nodes.rationale: The book's outline planned SARIMA losing to seasonal-naive as a gotcha; the real DM test instead found SARIMA beats both baselines decisively (p=0.0008 vs seasonal_naive, p=0.0019 vs naive) -- the test reports truth over storyline.
nodes.norm_label: honest reversal of the planned sarima-loses narrative

nodes.label: rolling_origin_backtest tool
nodes.rationale: Repeats a full fit_ets/fit_sarima/fit_gradient_boosted_trees call at several origins walking backward through the series, refitting from scratch each time; reports mean/std/per-origin performance as a stability measure. Excludes naive/seasonal-naive by design since they have no parameters to overfit or refit.

nodes.label: What a Bootstrap CI Can't Tell You
nodes.rationale: Bootstrap CIs resample points within one fixed holdout -- they capture luck within that window but not how much the metric would change if the holdout were a different stretch of the series entirely.
nodes.norm_label: what a bootstrap ci can't tell you

nodes.label: SARIMA(1,1,2) Cross-Origin Instability Finding
nodes.rationale: Real 5-origin rolling backtest: MAPE mean 4.04% (std 3.21) masked a nearly ten-fold swing (0.86% to 8.77%) across origins; relative spread 0.79 cleared the tool's own 0.5 instability threshold -- a good average sitting on top of a genuinely unstable model.

nodes.label: ts-deploy toolkit layer
nodes.rationale: Retrains on the entire cleaned series with nothing held back for deployment forecasts, since there is nothing left to score unseen dates against.

nodes.label: Silent Null-Propagation Failure
nodes.rationale: Deploying ETS straight from Chapter 3's uninterpolated mojito CSV (5 missing days) produced 30 null forecasts with no error field, and plausibility_check falsely reported the null result as unremarkable ('within the range of moves this series has made before'). The most dangerous failure mode in the book: a silent failure dressed up as a normal result.

nodes.label: plausibility_check
nodes.rationale: Not a hypothesis test -- a prompt for scrutiny, not a verdict. Checks only whether the forecast endpoint looks like a move the series has made before; a model that just replays an old pattern will pass this check almost by definition, even a silently broken null forecast passed it.

nodes.label: Full-Series Refit (no holdout)
nodes.rationale: The aicc reported here is not comparable to fit_ets/fit_sarima's backtest-split aicc for the same series, even though it's the same-named statistic, because training set size and fitted parameters differ.

nodes.label: Interval-Strategy Comparison (naive vs ETS vs SARIMA)
nodes.rationale: Real day-30 widths: seasonal-naive analytic 127.30, ETS simulated 326.95, SARIMA analytic 67.28 -- ETS's simulated interval was widest, contrary to the expectation that the naive floor would be dramatically widest. None of the three strategies is inherently the most honest; which is widest is empirical.
nodes.norm_label: interval-strategy comparison (naive vs ets vs sarima)

nodes.label: Recursive Multi-Step GBT Forecast
nodes.rationale: 90-day recursive GBT forecast on mojito inventory: interval barely widened (63.47 at day 5 vs 59.71 at day 90) despite compounding recursive risk; the model mostly re-plays the strongest repeating pattern (lag_7) rather than reasoning about the future, and its interval doesn't warn when that assumption breaks.

nodes.label: forecast_ensemble tool
nodes.rationale: Combines model intervals via sqrt(sum(w_i^2 sigma_i^2)), assuming independent errors. Legitimate only when a proper test (Diebold-Mariano) finds candidates genuinely statistically indistinguishable and robustness across them is wanted.

nodes.label: Ensemble Independence Assumption (not really true)
nodes.rationale: SARIMA and ETS were fit on the exact same 70 weeks of Death-Ray Revenue, so their errors share structure and are not independent; every combined interval in this chapter is a lower bound on true uncertainty, not a precise estimate.

nodes.label: Ensemble Arithmetic Verification (1/sqrt(2))
nodes.rationale: SARIMA fit twice identically at equal weight narrowed the interval by exactly 1/sqrt(2) (1556.803 to 1100.826, ratio 0.7071068), confirming the variance-combination formula works exactly as designed under the independence assumption.

nodes.label: Ensembling for the Wrong Reason, With Real Numbers
nodes.rationale: All 10 pairwise DM tests among naive/seasonal-naive/SARIMA/ETS/GBT came back significant, with ETS the outright winner (beats SARIMA at p=0.006) -- no legitimate tie to justify ensembling. Ensembling ETS+SARIMA anyway pulled the point forecast toward the worse model and mechanically widened ETS's well-calibrated interval (3,301.54) to 7,808.92, for no benefit.
nodes.norm_label: ensembling for the wrong reason, with real numbers

nodes.label: compare_forecast_to_actuals tool
nodes.rationale: At 10 matched days, 90% empirical coverage looked reassuring but carried a Wilson score 95% CI of [59.58%, 98.21%]; at 30 days the same 90% point estimate's CI narrowed to [74.38%, 96.54%] -- more data narrows the same point estimate's real uncertainty.

nodes.label: Residual-Outlier Check (modified z-score)
nodes.rationale: Flagged July 15 (the party, modified z=-9.05) and July 30 (an unremarkable end-of-horizon miss, z=-3.72). Excluding both dropped MAPE from 6.11% to 3.94%, but not every flagged date has a tidy explanation -- treating 'flagged' as synonymous with 'found the special event' overstates the case.

nodes.label: MAD-Zero-Collapse Failure Mode
nodes.rationale: Documented, real limitation: if half or more of a month's residuals are exactly identical, the median absolute deviation collapses to zero, and every point's modified z-score -- including a genuinely enormous 100-unit miss -- collapses to exactly 0.0, hiding the real outlier entirely. A real mathematical consequence of using median/MAD for robustness against a distribution where most values are identical.

nodes.label: detect_data_drift tool
nodes.rationale: Compares an 8-week recent window against a 26-week reference window (t-test, KS test, Cohen's d); drift_detected is a fixed p<0.05 rule with no exception for 'but it's probably just the trend' -- an ordinary, fully-expected trend produced drift_detected=true with Cohen's d=1.45 (conventionally 'large').

nodes.label: Trend-vs-Incident Ambiguity Finding
nodes.rationale: An ordinary trend produced Cohen's d=1.45 ('large' by textbook bins); a real incident (heist going public) on top of the same trend produced d=11.9146 -- both nominally 'large' by a fixed cutoff. Effect size gives a genuine magnitude scale, read comparatively against a series' own ordinary behavior, not sorted by a universal threshold. drift_detected being true says nothing about why the shift happened -- that's left to human/agent judgment, not the test.

nodes.label: rolling_drift_check tool
nodes.rationale: Five non-overlapping checks on the plain trending series all flagged drift (persistent_drift=true, 5/5) with values gently declining (3.03 to 1.45); on the escalation-shifted series the first four checks were numerically identical and only the last jumped to 11.91 -- the binary persistence verdict alone can't distinguish an ordinary sustained trend from an incident layered on top; the per-check trajectory can, localizing roughly when something new started.

nodes.label: recommend_retraining tool
nodes.rationale: Four fixed verdicts (retrain_now, investigate, monitor_closely, no_action_needed) from explicit error-degradation and drift thresholds. Real Interpol case: 1,218% degradation + drift -> retrain_now, unambiguous. A constructed close case (15% degradation, threshold 20%, CI [5%,25%] straddling the threshold) still returns no_action_needed deterministically but the reasoning explicitly flags the verdict as sensitive to sampling noise, not clean-cut.

nodes.label: compare_candidate_to_deployed tool
nodes.rationale: Answers 'is a freshly retrained candidate worth the disruption of redeploying' the same way every time via a fixed relative-improvement threshold, folding in confidence intervals on both candidate and deployed metrics using ratio-arithmetic corners, not variance addition.

nodes.label: should_redeploy Deterministic Gate (10% threshold)
nodes.rationale: Real refit ETS on rival-competition-flattened data got slightly worse (5.6156% vs deployed 5.5511%) -> should_redeploy=false. Real refit SARIMA(1,1,2) genuinely edged out the deployed model (5.3808% vs 5.5511%, 3.07% improvement) but the gate still returned false because 3.07% falls under the 10% threshold -- correctly rejecting a real, positive, but insufficient improvement to prevent churn from redeploying over noise-level gains.

nodes.label: Deployment Manifest (record_deployment / load_deployment_manifest)
nodes.rationale: load_deployment_manifest does no comparison of its own -- it just answers 'what's actually on file,' confirming the deployed model really is Chapter 9's ETS(add, mul, 7) at 5.5511% MAPE rather than a stale or misremembered number.

nodes.label: Ratio-Shaped Confidence Interval Arithmetic
nodes.rationale: pct_improvement = 100*(deployed-candidate)/deployed is a ratio, not a sum: its worst case pairs the candidate's highest plausible value with the deployed model's lowest, and its best case pairs the opposite corners -- verified exactly (22.22% to 54.55% when both sides carry a CI). This is genuinely different arithmetic from a symmetric variance-combination shortcut.

nodes.label: Rival Supervillain Price War Flattening Death-Ray Revenue
nodes.rationale: A real, genuine competitive-pressure regime change; neither a same-architecture ETS refit nor a SARIMA refit cleared the redeploy bar, leaving the competitive threat without a tidy resolution.
nodes.norm_label: rival supervillain price war flattening death-ray revenue

nodes.label: execute_redeploy tool
nodes.rationale: The one tool in the toolkit that changes anything real; refuses to act without confirmed=True (no default), and refuses a second, independent way when autonomous=True is passed without a standing authorization record on disk.

nodes.label: confirmed=True Gate
nodes.rationale: No default that takes action; real, demonstrated refusal returning status=not_executed every time it's omitted -- a deterministic gate, not a docstring promise.

nodes.label: Autonomous-Mode Authorization (authorize/check/revoke_autonomous_mode)
nodes.rationale: A real file on disk (self_destruct_autonomous.json) demonstrated live: authorize -> succeeds; revoke -> the identical call refuses again immediately, word for word, with no cached memory of prior approval. Stronger than an agent's conversational memory, which could be summarized away or adversarially manipulated. No expiry mechanism exists yet -- persists until explicitly revoked.

nodes.label: Deployment Manifest and Authorization Are Two Separate Files
nodes.rationale: record_deployment writes one file, authorize_autonomous_mode writes a different one, deliberately -- revoking autonomous-mode authorization did not touch the deployment manifest, since a deployment should persist across an autonomous-mode revocation. Keeps accidental coupling structurally impossible rather than merely discouraged.
nodes.norm_label: deployment manifest and authorization are two separate files

nodes.label: Prompt Craft Principles for Omen
nodes.rationale: Name the layer, carry forward whatever an earlier pipeline step already established, and leave genuine room for the agent to reason -- too little direction lets the agent fall back on arbitrary defaults, too much closes off reasoning entirely.

nodes.label: Prompt Failure Mode: Too Vague to Carry Anything Forward
nodes.rationale: 'Forecast the mojito inventory series' has no reason to avoid the raw, uninterpolated CSV or pick an informed model -- reproducing Chapter 14's documented silent null failure with no error message to catch it.
nodes.norm_label: prompt failure mode: too vague to carry anything forward

nodes.label: Prompt Failure Mode: So Specific It Leaves Nothing to Reason About
nodes.rationale: Hard-coding fit_sarima's exact winning order=[1,1,2] and requesting only aicc skips the verification step (checking the near-tied second candidate via DM test) that two full chapters established as necessary, and discards residual diagnostics and interval coverage computed for free.
nodes.norm_label: prompt failure mode: so specific it leaves nothing to reason about

nodes.label: Prompt Failure Mode: Right Question, Wrong Layer
nodes.rationale: 'Is SARIMA good enough to keep running?' maps onto three genuinely different, already-answered questions -- Layer 2 model selection (ETS beats SARIMA, p=0.006), Layer 4 production monitoring (compare_forecast_to_actuals/recommend_retraining), and Layer 5 redeploy decision (compare_candidate_to_deployed, 3.07% improvement rejected) -- that don't agree with each other; an agent must guess which was meant.
nodes.norm_label: prompt failure mode: right question, wrong layer

nodes.label: Deterministic Gate Principle
nodes.rationale: A decision belongs in a deterministic function exactly when the same inputs should produce the same answer every time regardless of who's asking or how the request was phrased; belongs to agent judgment exactly when that premise doesn't hold. Named thresholds are always inspectable/adjustable parameters, never hidden constants -- enforcement is deterministic, calibration is left visible.

nodes.label: Deliberately-Left-Open Agent Judgment Principle
nodes.rationale: Several places Omen went out of its way to NOT make deterministic even where it easily could have: which model family to try, whether search_sarima_orders should search d, what a drift flag means, whether a plausibility flag means real trouble, whether to ensemble at all.

nodes.label: Four-Question Trust Checklist
nodes.rationale: 1) Would identical inputs deserve an identical answer? 2) Is the action hard to reverse or does it touch shared/production state? 3) Does the rule require a judgment call about where to set the bar? 4) Could the same test result mean genuinely different things depending on context a fixed function can't see?

nodes.label: Omen's Five-Layer Pipeline (ts-analyst, ts-forecaster, ts-deploy, ts-monitor, ts-retrain)
nodes.rationale: Each layer's real, chapter-by-chapter track record: ts-analyst found n_missing=5 and grounded d=1; ts-forecaster showed AIC isn't the best-forecast signal and GBT feature importance isn't causation; ts-deploy turned the missing-data finding into a hard consequence and revealed three different honest interval widths; ts-monitor separated a real party-night anomaly from a marginal unexplained miss and an ordinary-trend-vs-incident ambiguity; ts-retrain rejected a real but insufficient improvement and used ratio-shaped CI arithmetic.
nodes.norm_label: omen's five-layer pipeline (ts-analyst, ts-forecaster, ts-deploy, ts-monitor, ts-retrain)

nodes.label: Omen's Genuinely Open Items
nodes.rationale: No expiry on autonomous-mode grants (persists until explicitly revoked); not yet published to PyPI (distribution name omen-agentic-forecasting settled, importable module stays omen, no release pushed).

nodes.label: Omen (agentic time-series forecasting toolkit)
nodes.rationale: The book's whole method: build the deterministic gate where the answer shouldn't depend on who's asking, leave reasoning to whoever can see context a fixed rule can't, and verify the real number before writing it down.

nodes.norm_label: sarima order search: candidates ranked by aicc (bar chart)
